claims.claim_text: The ValuKredit fintech anecdote is presented as a real March 2022 incident in Berlin.
claims.span_hint: Why This Module Matters, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: March 2022. A mid-sized fintech startup in Berlin named ValuKredit was expanding rapidly.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A platform team running self-managed Kubernetes can accumulate undocumented operational knowledge that becomes a major risk when key engineers leave.
claims.rationale: This names a company, place, and date without a citable primary source.

claims.claim_text: The module claims the named startup left a kube-apiserver CVE unpatched for eleven days and faced $3.4 million in breach liability exposure.
claims.span_hint: Why This Module Matters, ValuKredit anecdote
claims.anchor_text: When a critical CVE hit the kube-apiserver in April, the depleted team simply froze. They lacked the operational confidence to patch the control plane safely. For eleven agonizing days, their production cluster ran a publicly disclosed, highly weaponized vulnerability because nobody understood the upgrade procedure well enough to execute it without risking a total outage. The subsequent postmortem estimated their risk exposure at three point four million dollars in potential breach liability.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When a serious control-plane vulnerability appears, a team without clear upgrade runbooks and enough trained operators can end up delaying patching because they fear causing an outage.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident narrative with fake-precise timing and dollar figures.

claims.claim_text: The named startup supposedly migrated entirely to GKE six months later and returned engineers to product work.
claims.span_hint: Why This Module Matters, ValuKredit anecdote ending
claims.anchor_text: Six months later, they abandoned their custom setup and migrated entirely to GKE. Their remaining engineers finally returned to shipping product features instead of babysitting etcd databases.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Eventually, some teams decide to move from self-managed clusters to a managed service so their engineers can spend less time on control-plane operations and more time on product work.
claims.rationale: This continues the fabricated company-specific timeline and outcome.

claims.claim_text: The global logistics company EKS anecdote presents a real incident with specific SLA, workload, and cost outcomes.
claims.span_hint: Why This Module Matters, second anecdote
claims.anchor_text: A global logistics company running exclusively on EKS discovered that their managed control plane's fifteen-minute SLA for API server availability was woefully insufficient for their real-time, automated container orchestration workloads. They required sub-second failover and custom admission webhooks that inherently conflicted with EKS's strict managed add-on lifecycle policies. Ultimately, they moved to self-managed Kubernetes on raw EC2 instances. Consequently, their infrastructure costs dropped by a massive margin because they could meticulously right-size their control plane nodes instead of paying flat per-cluster fees, and they regained absolute authority over their scheduler.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In a smaller set of cases, teams decide managed Kubernetes is not a fit because they need tighter control over scheduler behavior, cluster topology, or failure characteristics than the managed service allows.
claims.rationale: This is another unsourced named-industry war story with precise claims that are not attributable.

claims.claim_text: Managed Kubernetes follows a shared responsibility model, and the exact boundary differs by provider and operating mode.
claims.span_hint: Section 1: The Shared Responsibility Model, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The GKE primary doc directly supports the shared-responsibility concept, but one page does not fully substantiate the three-provider comparison.

claims.claim_text: Even with managed Kubernetes, customers still own major security and operations responsibilities, but the sentence overstates worker-node responsibility as universal across providers and modes.
claims.span_hint: Section 1, after Shared Responsibility table
claims.anchor_text: Notice that even with a fully managed Kubernetes cluster, you are still actively responsible for a massive portion of the operational stack. Worker node OS patching, network policies, pod security admission, and ingress controller configuration—these remain your unyielding responsibility regardless of the cloud provider you choose.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Notice that even with a managed Kubernetes cluster, you still retain major responsibilities for workload security, networking, configuration, and often the timing or initiation of node updates, depending on the provider and cluster mode you choose.
claims.rationale: The teaching point is valid, but the original wording is too absolute across Autopilot, Automatic, Fargate, and standard managed-node modes.

claims.claim_text: EKS control planes run in AWS-managed infrastructure, and AWS provisions cross-account ENIs in customer subnets so the control plane can communicate with nodes.
claims.span_hint: Section 2: Amazon EKS Architecture
claims.rationale: The EKS API reference explicitly states the control plane runs in an AWS-managed account and that AWS provisions ENIs in customer subnets.

claims.claim_text: The module says EKS etcd is 'continuously backed up and encrypted by AWS'.
claims.span_hint: Section 2: Amazon EKS Architecture, Key Architectural Details
claims.anchor_text: You NEVER see, touch, or interact with the etcd instances. They are continuously backed up and encrypted by AWS.
claims.suggested_rewrite: You do not get direct access to the EKS control-plane data store; AWS manages that layer for you and encrypts control-plane data at rest.
claims.rationale: The encryption part is documented, but the blanket 'continuously backed up' phrasing is not directly supported by the cited primary docs.

claims.claim_text: GKE Autopilot manages worker nodes and generally bills based on the resources workloads request.
claims.span_hint: Section 2: Google GKE Architecture, Key Architectural Details
claims.rationale: The Autopilot overview directly describes Google-managed nodes and workload-request-based pricing.

claims.claim_text: GKE cluster state can be stored in Spanner rather than etcd on control-plane VMs in some implementations.
claims.span_hint: Section 2: Google GKE Architecture, Key Architectural Details
claims.rationale: The cluster architecture doc explicitly says GKE clusters store state in either etcd or Spanner.

claims.claim_text: AKS Free has no SLA, while Standard and Premium include uptime SLA coverage.
claims.span_hint: Section 2: Azure AKS Architecture, Key Architectural Details
claims.rationale: The AKS pricing-tier doc directly distinguishes Free from SLA-backed Standard and Premium tiers.

claims.claim_text: The control-plane cost comparison row is outdated and inaccurate, especially for GKE and AKS.
claims.span_hint: Section 2: The Critical Differences table
claims.anchor_text: | Control Plane Cost | $0.10/hr ($73/mo) | Free (Autopilot) or $0.10/hr (Standard) | Free (no SLA) or $0.10/hr (SLA) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Control Plane Cost | Separate cluster-management fee applies | Cluster-management fee applies, with limited monthly free-tier credits in some modes | Free and paid management tiers are available |
claims.rationale: Current vendor pricing pages no longer support this one-line table as written.

claims.claim_text: The control-plane SLA row is oversimplified because GKE and AKS SLAs depend on cluster type, tier, and zone topology.
claims.span_hint: Section 2: The Critical Differences table
claims.anchor_text: | Control Plane SLA | 99.95% | 99.95% (Regional) | 99.95% (Standard tier) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Control Plane SLA | SLA-backed on standard EKS control planes | Depends on cluster type, with higher guarantees for regional and Autopilot control planes than zonal Standard clusters | Depends on tier and availability-zone configuration |
claims.rationale: The raw numbers are not universally true for all GKE and AKS modes represented in the row.

claims.claim_text: The max-pods-per-node row is too simplistic because each provider's defaults and limits vary by networking mode, instance size, and cluster mode.
claims.span_hint: Section 2: The Critical Differences table
claims.anchor_text: | Max Pods per Node | 110 (default ENI limits) | 110 (default), 256 (GKE) | 250 |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Max Pods per Node | Depends on instance type and networking configuration | Defaults and limits vary by cluster mode and Pod CIDR settings | Depends on network plugin and cluster configuration |
claims.rationale: Official docs show more nuanced defaults and caps than this row suggests.

claims.claim_text: The provider version-lag row gives unstable, provider-specific timing numbers that drift over time.
claims.span_hint: Section 2: The Critical Differences table
claims.anchor_text: | K8s Version Lag | ~2-3 months behind upstream | ~1-2 months behind upstream | ~2-3 months behind upstream |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | K8s Version Lag | Provider release cadence, not upstream-day-zero | Provider release cadence and release channel determine timing | Provider release cadence and regional rollout determine timing |
claims.rationale: Release timing is real, but the exact month ranges are unstable and not safely supportable as evergreen table data.

claims.claim_text: The 'Autopilot Mode' row is inaccurate because AWS and Azure now have higher-abstraction managed modes that are not represented correctly.
claims.span_hint: Section 2: The Critical Differences table
claims.anchor_text: | Autopilot Mode | Fargate (serverless pods) | GKE Autopilot (full cluster) | None (virtual nodes via ACI) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Autopilot / Higher-Abstraction Mode | EKS offers Fargate and Auto Mode, which are not identical to full-cluster autopilot semantics | GKE Autopilot is the clearest full-cluster autopilot-style mode here | AKS offers Automatic mode rather than having no higher-abstraction option |
claims.rationale: The original row is no longer an accurate cross-provider comparison.

claims.claim_text: The TCO tables are presented as realistic annual numbers, but they are illustrative estimates rather than source-backed market facts.
claims.span_hint: Section 3: Total Cost of Ownership, opening sentence
claims.anchor_text: Let us build a highly realistic Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) model for a medium-complexity production deployment.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Let us build an illustrative Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) model with round numbers for a medium-complexity production deployment.
claims.rationale: The lesson is valid, but the figures are modeling assumptions, not directly citable market facts.

claims.claim_text: The module states that managed Kubernetes is roughly 60% cheaper.
claims.span_hint: Section 3, after TCO tables
claims.anchor_text: The managed option is roughly sixty percent cheaper when you accurately account for raw labor and enterprise risk.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Depending on staffing, operational maturity, and risk tolerance, managed Kubernetes can be materially cheaper than it first appears once you include labor and operational risk.
claims.rationale: That percentage is entirely dependent on the module's illustrative assumptions.

claims.claim_text: The hidden-cost row says etcd backup storage is simply 'included' for managed Kubernetes.
claims.span_hint: Section 3: The Costs People Forget table
claims.anchor_text: | etcd backup storage | You build + pay | Included |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | etcd backup storage | You design, operate, and pay for backups yourself | Control-plane data protection is provider-managed, but backup and restore capabilities vary by service |
claims.rationale: Managed offerings abstract the control-plane datastore, but 'included' overstates a provider-consistent backup story.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes ships minor releases regularly and only supports a limited window of recent versions.
claims.span_hint: Section 4: Version Lifecycle, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Kubernetes releases three minor versions per year, approximately every fifteen weeks. Each version is officially supported for only about fourteen months.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kubernetes ships minor releases regularly and only supports a sliding window of recent versions, so teams need an ongoing upgrade plan.
claims.rationale: The specific cadence and duration numbers are approximations that drift over time and are better removed than weakly anchored.

claims.claim_text: EKS has its own version lifecycle with extended support and eventual auto-upgrade behavior at support boundaries.
claims.span_hint: Section 4: Provider Upgrade Policies, EKS bullet
claims.anchor_text: - **EKS**: Adds a new version approximately two to three months after the upstream release. Gives advance notice before initiating a forced upgrade. Extended support is available for an additional twelve months at a heavy premium.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **EKS**: Follows its own version lifecycle, provides advance notice around support deadlines, and offers an extended-support window for clusters that stay on an older minor version longer.
claims.rationale: The lifecycle is real, but the exact lag claim is not a stable evergreen fact.

claims.claim_text: GKE release channels drive auto-upgrade behavior, and channel choice affects how quickly clusters receive new versions.
claims.span_hint: Section 4: Provider Upgrade Policies, GKE bullet
claims.rationale: The release-channels doc directly explains Rapid, Regular, Stable, and default auto-upgrade behavior.

claims.claim_text: AKS supports the latest GA Kubernetes minor version plus the two previous GA minor versions, with gradual rollout across regions.
claims.span_hint: Section 4: Provider Upgrade Policies, AKS bullet
claims.anchor_text: - **AKS**: Operates on an "N-2" support model (the latest release and the two previous minor versions). Preview versions are generally available earlier for testing.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **AKS**: Supports the latest GA minor version plus the previous two GA minor versions, and new versions are rolled out gradually across regions.
claims.rationale: The N-2 support model is documented, but the preview wording is looser than the primary support doc supports.

claims.claim_text: A single etcd compaction mistake can render the entire cluster 'irrevocably' read-only.
claims.span_hint: Section 4: Self-Managed Upgrade Reality, narrative after command block
claims.anchor_text: A single mistake during etcd compaction can render the entire cluster irrevocably read-only.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A mistake during etcd maintenance can make the control plane unavailable or force a time-consuming recovery procedure.
claims.rationale: The operational risk is real, but the original wording is too absolute and not safely supportable as stated.

claims.claim_text: The GKE 'Did You Know' bullet says GKE was the first managed Kubernetes service and ties that directly to Borg since 2003.
claims.span_hint: Did You Know, bullet 1
claims.anchor_text: - **GKE was the very first managed Kubernetes service**, officially launched in 2015—just a single year after Kubernetes itself was open-sourced by Google. Google had already been orchestrating massive container workloads internally via Borg since 2003, giving them a monumental head start that is still evident in GKE's rapid feature velocity today.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **GKE has been generally available since 2015**, and it reflects Google's earlier experience operating large-scale cluster-management systems before Kubernetes.
claims.rationale: The general history is real, but the bundled 'first managed service' and 'since 2003' framing is too specific for the cited allowlist.

claims.claim_text: EKS control planes run on AWS-managed EC2 infrastructure, and cross-account ENIs plus VPC-native pod IPs can contribute to subnet exhaustion.
claims.span_hint: Did You Know bullet 2; repeated in Quiz 5 answer
claims.rationale: AWS docs explicitly describe the AWS-managed VPC, X-ENIs in customer subnets, and VPC design implications.

claims.claim_text: The AKS 'Did You Know' bullet says AKS is the only major managed service with a free tier, then adds an unsupported normative claim about 'professional negligence'.
claims.span_hint: Did You Know, bullet 3
claims.anchor_text: - **AKS is the only major managed Kubernetes service that provides a genuinely free tier with absolutely no time limits.** The significant caveat: the free tier provides zero SLA. If your control plane fails, Azure's default response is to suggest upgrading to the Standard tier. Running mission-critical production workloads on a free-tier AKS cluster is professional negligence.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **AKS offers a Free tier with no SLA and paid tiers with uptime SLAs.** The Free tier is suitable for development and evaluation, while production workloads should use an SLA-backed tier.
claims.rationale: The first sentence is too broad across the market, and the later sentence is editorial rather than a citable fact.

claims.claim_text: The etcd 'Did You Know' bullet says etcd predates Kubernetes, uses Raft, and losing quorum makes the cluster read-only.
claims.span_hint: Did You Know, bullet 4
claims.rationale: The canonical source for the quorum behavior is the official etcd documentation, which is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The solution section says Kubernetes v1.32.6 is three minor versions behind current.
claims.span_hint: Hands-On Exercise, Task 1 solution, risk analysis item 1
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes releases page shows 1.35, 1.34, and 1.33 as current supported minors, making 1.32 three minors behind.

claims.claim_text: The solution section says Ubuntu 20.04 LTS reached end of standard support in May 2025.
claims.span_hint: Hands-On Exercise, Task 1 solution, risk analysis item 2
claims.rationale: Canonical's official lifecycle page supports the date, but `ubuntu.com` is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A /24 subnet can be exhausted on EKS because AWS-managed ENIs and VPC-native pod IP allocation consume addresses in addition to visible nodes.
claims.span_hint: Quiz 5 answer
claims.rationale: AWS networking docs directly describe cross-account ENIs and the VPC/IP planning consequences.

claims.claim_text: The GKE Stable-channel answer frames the 3-4 month lag as intentional qualification time and says supported versions continue to receive security patches.
claims.span_hint: Quiz 6 answer
claims.anchor_text: Being 3-4 months behind means you are running versions that have been thoroughly battle-tested by users in the Rapid and Regular channels first, catching edge-case bugs before they hit your production workloads. Switching to self-managed to run the bleeding-edge version would massively increase operational risk and the burden of patching. Furthermore, managed providers actively backport critical security CVE patches to the Stable channel versions, meaning your cluster remains secure even if you aren't on the latest feature release.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The Stable channel intentionally trails earlier channels so production clusters run versions that have had more qualification time, and GKE continues to deliver supported security patches for those versions.
claims.rationale: The underlying lesson is supported, but the exact month figure and explanatory framing are better softened.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Version Skew Policy
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains the supported version window and the control-plane-to-kubelet compatibility rules that drive upgrade planning.

further_reading.title: Amazon EKS VPC and Subnet Considerations
further_reading.why_relevant: Shows how EKS control-plane networking works and why ENIs and VPC-native pod IPs matter for subnet design.

further_reading.title: GKE Release Channels
further_reading.why_relevant: Clarifies how Rapid, Regular, Stable, and auto-upgrade behavior affect managed-cluster version lifecycle decisions.

further_reading.title: AKS Free, Standard, and Premium Pricing Tiers
further_reading.why_relevant: Defines what Azure actually manages in each AKS tier and where SLA-backed production guidance begins.

notes: Several comparison-table rows collapse provider-specific nuance too aggressively and should be rewritten rather than cited. The TCO sections and named company anecdotes read as illustrative teaching material, not source-backed historical fact, so they should be reframed explicitly or stripped of false precision.
